title: Baseline retest (no implementation change)
motivation:
Used by the orchestrator integration test (--from-hypothesis path).
Measures the unchanged baseline through the full eval pipeline so the
resulting log entry locks in the first fitness number for phase 8.
hypothesis:
No RTL changes — exercises the worktree -> emit_verilog -> formal ->
cosim -> fpga path on the existing rtl/*.sv to confirm the orchestrator
produces a structured log entry.

changes.description:
Sentinel — no real change. skip_implementation:true below bypasses
the implementation agent, so the file path here is metadata only;
it just has to satisfy the schema's rtl/<file> pattern. core.sv is
always present and untouched.

title: Move DIV/REM off the single-cycle ALU path
motivation:
The baseline ALU places RV32M multiplication and division in the same
combinational EX-stage result mux as common integer operations. A 32-bit
combinational divider is very likely to dominate LUT depth and area on
the Tang Nano 20K, yet CoreMark's timed region is matrix/list/state code
that is multiply-heavy and effectively division-free. Keeping DIV/REM
single-cycle optimizes an uncommon path while lowering Fmax for every
instruction.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU cases in rtl/alu.sv with a
small iterative RV32M divide/remainder unit and add an EX-stage interlock
only when one of those opcodes is in flight. Leave ADD/SUB/logic/shift,
branches, loads/stores, jumps, and MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU on the current
single-cycle path. The timed CoreMark loop should pay no meaningful IPC
penalty because it does not execute DIV/REM, while the FPGA critical path
should shrink from divider-through-ALU-mux to the existing add/compare/mul
paths. Preserve exact RV32M divide-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 semantics and
keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions for formal builds.

changes.description:
Remove real combinational DIV/REM hardware from the normal ALU result
mux, retaining single-cycle non-divide ALU and MUL behavior plus the
existing ALTOPS formulas under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS.

Add an iterative signed/unsigned divider/remainder block with start,
busy, done, result, and operation inputs, implementing all RV32M corner
cases before or after the unsigned divide iteration as appropriate.

Detect DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU in EX, start the iterative unit, hold the
ID/EX-to-EX/MEM advancement until done, then write the completed result
into the EX/MEM register with the original instruction metadata.

Add the divide-unit busy/done interlock to the existing stall network so
younger instructions, fetch, and RVFI retirement remain ordered while a
multi-cycle DIV/REM is completing.

Wire the new EX-stage divide stall signal into the top-level hazard
controls without changing the core IO shape or the single-retire RVFI
channel-0 convention.

title: Elastic Fetch Skid Buffer
motivation:
The baseline frontend has no real IF/ID storage: instruction data flows
directly from io_imemData into decode, and hazard_unit globally freezes the
PC on load-use, dmem stalls, and imem stalls. Under the tournament's ~22%
random imem backpressure, every not-ready fetch becomes a visible decode
bubble. Under dmem backpressure, the independent imem port is also idle even
though it could be fetching sequential instructions for after the stall.
CoreMark's IPC is already close to the single-issue limit, so the best
structural win is to recover wasted frontend cycles without widening the
core or lengthening the EX critical path.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational IF/ID handoff with an elastic 2-entry fetch skid
buffer that decouples instruction fetch from decode. The fetch side advances
the PC and captures sequential instructions whenever io_imemReady is high
and the buffer is not full, even while ID/EX is held by a dmem stall. The
decode side consumes one buffered instruction per non-stalled cycle. Preserve
the baseline zero-latency behavior with an empty-buffer bypass from
io_imemData to decode when the backend is ready and imem is ready, so the
no-stall path and branch target refill latency do not regress. On EX
redirect, flush all queued entries and restart fetch at redirect_target.
This should hide a meaningful fraction of random imem stalls and avoid
wasting fetch bandwidth during backend stalls while keeping the core
single-issue, in-order, and RVFI channel-0-only.

changes.description:
Add a two-entry valid/ready fetch queue with a zero-latency bypass when
the queue is empty. Push fetched {pc,instr} entries on imemReady while
space is available, pop only when decode actually accepts the current
instruction, and flush the queue on reset or redirect.

Stop treating raw io_imemReady as a global pipeline stall. Keep load-use
and dmem interlocks, but let an empty fetch buffer naturally present
valid=0/NOP to ID while the fetch side waits for imemReady.

Wire the frontend consume/full/empty controls between if_stage and the
existing stall/flush network without changing the top-level core IO or
the NRET=2 RVFI port convention.

If needed, extend the IF/ID bundle or add small fetch-buffer typedefs for
queued pc/instruction/valid metadata; no architectural state or RVFI
field widths change.

implementation_notes:
# Elastic Fetch Skid Buffer

## What changed

- Reworked `rtl/if_stage.sv` into a two-entry elastic fetch skid buffer.
  - Empty-buffer bypass preserves the baseline zero-wait path from `io_imemData` to decode.
  - Fetch advances `io_imemAddr` when `io_imemReady` is high and the buffer has room, including while decode is blocked by load-use or dmem interlocks.
  - Decode pops one queued instruction only when the ID stage actually accepts the presented IF/ID item.
  - Reset, flush, and EX redirect clear queued entries; redirect restarts fetch at `redirect_target`.
- Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` so raw `io_imemReady` no longer creates a global pipeline stall. The hazard unit now keeps only load-use and dmem interlocks.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` wiring to pass `io_imemReady` into IF and remove it from the hazard unit.
- Added `rtl/000_core_pkg.sv`, an early-sorted guarded mirror of `core_pkg.sv`, so the required `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` command sees typedefs/localparams before alphabetically later RTL consumers. Builds that explicitly read `core_pkg.sv` first see the mirror as a no-op through the shared `CORE_PKG_DEFINED` guard.

## Deviations

- I kept the existing five-stage topology and reused `stall_if` as the IF-side decode backpressure signal rather than adding new top-level internal control names.
- During a dmem stall with a redirecting instruction held in ID/EX, IF still treats the continuously asserted redirect as a squash. This preserves correctness but does not prefetch past that held redirect.

## Concerns

- `000_core_pkg.sv` duplicates declarations from `core_pkg.sv` to satisfy the fixed lint glob order. If `core_pkg.sv` changes later, the mirror must be kept in sync.

## Local status

- Lint: pass with `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`.
- Formal: pass, 105 passed / 0 failed, run from a writable `/tmp/codex-formal-hyp-20260427-002-r1s1` copy because the worktree's `formal/riscv-formal` path is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable sandbox. A direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in this sandbox failed before checks with `Operation not permitted` while clearing the staged core directory.

title: Backward-Branch Taken Predictor
motivation: The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX and fetches strictly pc+4 until that resolution, so every taken loop branch in CoreMark pays a two-instruction wrong-path flush. CoreMark's hot loops are dominated by short backward conditional branches whose direction is highly biased toward taken. A small static predictor can remove most loop-back redirect bubbles without adding a learned-state table, RAM, or speculative architectural state.
hypothesis: Add a decode-light static branch predictor in IF that recognizes B-type instructions and predicts backward branches taken when the sign bit of the branch immediate is set. Compute the B immediate in IF, redirect the next PC to pc+imm for predicted-taken backward branches, and carry predicted_taken plus predicted_target through IF/ID and ID/EX. In EX, compare the actual branch result and target against the prediction, redirect only on mispredict, and set rvfi pc_wdata to the actual next PC as today. JAL/JALR remain resolved in EX to keep the predictor slot focused on conditional branches and avoid adding a return-address stack or BTB.

changes.description: Extend IF/ID and ID/EX pipeline bundles with predicted_taken and predicted_target fields so EX can distinguish correct predictions from true mispredicts while preserving RVFI pc_wdata semantics.

Add static backward-branch-taken prediction for B-type instructions: decode the branch immediate from the fetched instruction, select the predicted next PC for the PC register, and emit the prediction metadata with the fetched instruction. Suppress prediction when reset, stall, flush, or an EX redirect is active.

Change redirect generation from always-on taken-branch redirect to mispredict-only redirect. Actual next PC remains pc+4, branch target, or jump target as appropriate, and misalignment traps still suppress redirect and mark the retiring instruction illegal.
Treat EX redirect as a mispredict recovery signal and keep the existing IF/ID and ID/EX flush behavior for wrong-path instructions. No invariant checks or RVFI channel behavior are weakened.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added `predicted_taken` and `predicted_target` metadata to the IF/ID and ID/EX pipeline bundles.
- Added an IF-stage static predictor for legal B-type conditional branches with a negative immediate. Predicted-taken branches update the fetch PC to `pc + imm` and carry the prediction metadata with the instruction.
- Changed EX redirect generation so conditional branches redirect only when the resolved next PC differs from the predicted next PC. JAL/JALR still redirect from EX, and RVFI `pc_wdata` still reports the resolved architectural next PC.
- Added `rtl/00_core_pkg_first.sv` so the required raw `rtl/*.sv` Verilator lint command sees `core_pkg.sv` definitions before `core.sv`. Normal ordered builds read `core_pkg.sv` first, so this shim is a guard-protected no-op there.

## Deviations

- IF prediction is suppressed for reserved branch funct3 values and for predicted targets with `target[1:0] != 2'b00`. This keeps illegal branch encodings and branch-target misalignment traps on the existing fallthrough/trap path instead of speculatively fetching from a path EX is required not to redirect to.

## Concerns

- The predictor intentionally does not handle JAL/JALR, forward taken branches, or any learned state, matching the hypothesis scope.
- The extra `pc + B-imm` adder is now in IF. That may affect Fmax; the FPGA gate will determine whether the IPC gain outweighs it.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree was blocked by sandbox permissions because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable roots. I staged an equivalent temporary project under `/tmp/hyp-formal.rMtIYN` with the current RTL and ran the unmodified script there.
- Temporary formal run: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Single Shared RV32M Multiplier
motivation:
The current ALU builds three independent wide products every cycle:
signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, and signed*unsigned. Those products feed
the same EX-stage result mux as ordinary integer operations, so their area and
routing pressure can lower Fmax even when most instructions are ADD/logic,
branches, loads, and stores. CoreMark does use MUL, so making multiplication
multi-cycle risks losing IPC; however all RV32M multiply variants can be
implemented by one signedness-controlled 33x33 product with no latency change.
This is a narrower and lower-risk micro-optimization than the prior failed
DIV/REM interlock attempt because it leaves divider behavior, pipeline stalls,
and RVFI ordering untouched.
hypothesis:
Replace the three unconditional multiplier expressions in rtl/alu.sv with a
single shared 33-bit-by-33-bit product. For MUL, zero-extend both operands and
use the low 32 bits; for MULH, sign-extend both operands; for MULHSU,
sign-extend rs1 and zero-extend rs2; for MULHU, zero-extend both operands.
Select product[63:32] for the high-half operations and product[31:0] for MUL.
Keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions exactly as they are and leave
DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU single-cycle for this experiment. The expected win is lower
LUT/DSP use and less EX-stage mux/routing pressure, improving median Fmax
without changing CoreMark instruction count, branch behavior, memory behavior,
or the single-retire RVFI contract.

changes.description:
Replace mul_ss, mul_uu, and mul_su with one signed 33x33 product whose
operand sign-extension is selected from the ALU multiply opcode. Route the
existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU cases from that shared product, preserve
exact RV32M bit results, keep ALTOPS formulas unchanged, and do not change
DIV/REM implementation or any pipeline control.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Replaced the three independent multiplier products in `rtl/alu.sv` with one shared multiplier path.
- Added per-operand signedness selection for RV32M multiply operations:
  - `MUL` and `MULHU`: zero-extend both operands.
  - `MULH`: sign-extend both operands.
  - `MULHSU`: sign-extend `rs1` / operand `a`, zero-extend `rs2` / operand `b`.
- The shared product uses signed 33-bit operands and keeps the low 64 product bits; `MUL` selects `[31:0]`, and the high-half multiply operations select `[63:32]`.
- Left the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` substitution formulas unchanged.
- Left `DIV`, `DIVU`, `REM`, and `REMU` unchanged and single-cycle.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis plan.
- The product signal is stored as 64 bits rather than 66 bits because RV32M only needs product bits `[63:0]`; the 33-bit input extension still preserves the correct signed/unsigned 32x32 result bits.

## Concerns

- No known RTL concerns. The multiplier remains combinational, so latency and pipeline control are unchanged.
- Running `bash formal/run_all.sh` directly in the worktree failed before elaboration because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected path and the script could not remove staged `.sv` files there (`Operation not permitted`). I reran the same script from a `/tmp` copy with the symlink dereferenced.

## Verification

- PASS: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`
- PASS: `bash formal/run_all.sh` from `/tmp/alu-formal-run.hRBUSw` after copying current `rtl/` and dereferencing `formal/riscv-formal`; final tally was `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- PASS: `pytest -q test/test_alu.py`

title: MEM-to-EX Load-Use Bypass
motivation: The current five-stage pipeline still has the textbook structural load-use gap: a load in EX always forces the immediately following consumer to wait one extra cycle, even when the dmem bus is ready and the loaded word is already available combinationally in MEM. CoreMark's list, matrix, and state kernels contain many load-to-use pairs, so this fixed bubble directly lowers iterations per cycle. The recent predictor win improved control flow without changing the backend; the next most promising structural angle is to add the missing MEM-to-EX load-data path and remove the mandatory interlock while preserving the single-issue in-order retirement contract.
hypothesis: Add a direct load-result forwarding path from the MEM stage's existing sign/zero-extended load_data to the EX operand muxes. When an instruction in EX consumes the rd of a load currently in MEM, select this MEM load result for rs1 and/or rs2 instead of stalling for the value to appear in MEM/WB. If io_dmemReady is low for that load, keep the current dmem hold behavior and suppress any younger EX redirect until the load data is valid; once ready, the dependent instruction executes in the same cycle the load retires. Misaligned or trapping loads must not forward and must still suppress rd writes. This should recover load-use bubbles in the zero-wait and random-stall CoreMark runs without widening fetch, decode, or RVFI retirement.

changes.description: Expose a combinational load-forward result and validity bit from the existing load extraction logic. The valid bit should require an in-flight legal load, dmem readiness, no memory misalignment trap, and a nonzero destination that will actually write back.

Teach the forwarding selector to distinguish EX/MEM ALU results from EX/MEM loads. Do not forward the load's address as an ALU result; instead choose a new MEM-load forwarding source when the consumer matches the load rd and the MEM-stage load-forward valid bit is asserted.

Add the MEM-load forwarding input to both rs1 and rs2 operand muxes so ALU operations, branch comparisons, JALR bases, and store data can consume an immediately preceding load. Gate redirect generation while a selected load-forward source is waiting on dmemReady so a dependent branch cannot redirect using stale operands.

Remove the unconditional one-cycle load-use bubble from stall_if, stall_id, and flush_id. Keep the existing dmem stall/hold behavior for loads whose data is not ready, and continue clearing MEM/WB valid during held cycles to avoid duplicate retirement or duplicate regfile writes.

Wire the MEM-stage load-forward data and valid signal into the forwarding and EX-stage logic while preserving the top-level core IO shape, single retirement on RVFI channel 0, and tied-off RVFI channel 1 convention.

implementation_notes:
## MEM-to-EX Load-Use Bypass

### What changed

- Added MEM-stage combinational load forwarding outputs in `rtl/mem_stage.sv`:
  `load_fwd_data`, `load_fwd_valid`, and `load_fwd_wait`.
- `load_fwd_valid` requires a valid legal load, `dmem_ready`, no memory
  misalignment trap, nonzero `rd`, and a register write that will actually
  reach writeback.
- Updated `rtl/forward_unit.sv` to distinguish EX/MEM ALU producers from
  EX/MEM loads. Loads no longer forward their address as an ALU result; they
  select the new MEM load source when the data is valid or when the dependent
  EX instruction is being held waiting for dmem.
- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv` operand muxes with the MEM load source. This covers
  ALU operands, branch comparisons, JALR base operands, and store data.
- Gated EX redirect only when a branch operand or JALR base selects the MEM load
  source while the load data is still waiting on `dmem_ready`.
- Removed the unconditional load-use interlock from `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`; the
  remaining stalls are imem backpressure and dmem hold behavior.
- Wired the new signals through `rtl/core.sv`.

### Deviations

- Kept a separate `load_fwd_wait` signal in addition to the requested valid bit.
  This lets the forwarding selector mark the dependency while the dmem bus is
  not ready, so EX can suppress dependent redirects without falling back to an
  older MEM/WB value.
- The redirect wait gate is limited to branch operands and JALR's rs1 operand.
  This avoids unnecessarily delaying an unrelated JAL when immediate bits happen
  to match the MEM load destination.

### Concerns

- No new RTL testbench was added. The change is covered by lint and the existing
  riscv-formal gate, but random-stall cosim remains the main validation for the
  `load_fwd_wait` behavior under dmem backpressure.
- The existing hazard unit still treats any EX/MEM memory operation as a dmem
  hold candidate, matching the prior behavior.

### Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked by sandbox permissions because
  `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink outside this worktree and the script stages
  files under that target.
- Equivalent formal run from a writable `/tmp` copy of `rtl/` and
  `formal/riscv-formal`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: IF Direct-Jump Predictor
motivation:
The current best RTL already predicts backward conditional branches in IF,
but ex_stage still asserts a redirect for every JAL/JALR. That leaves
unconditional direct jumps, calls, and compiler-generated `j` control-flow
skips paying the same wrong-path flush as an unpredicted taken branch, even
though a JAL target is fully encoded in the fetched instruction. CoreMark's
optimized RV32IM image contains many direct JAL/J sites, so eliminating those
redirect bubbles should improve iterations/cycle with much less timing risk
than adding a BTB or learned direction table to the IF path.
hypothesis:
Extend the existing IF-stage predictor from backward conditional branches to
direct JAL targets. Recognize opcode 1101111 in IF, form the J immediate,
predict pc+imm as the next PC when the target is word-aligned, and carry the
existing predicted_taken/predicted_target metadata with the instruction. In
EX, make JAL redirect mispredict-only by comparing the resolved jump target
against the carried prediction; JALR remains unpredicted and still redirects
from EX. This should remove the two-cycle recovery penalty from correctly
predicted direct jumps and calls while preserving RVFI pc_wdata, misaligned
jump traps, and the single-retire channel-0 convention.

changes.description:
Add JAL recognition beside the existing B-type predictor, compute the
J-immediate target with the current PC, and choose that target for the
next PC when prediction is enabled and the target is word-aligned. Emit
predicted_taken and predicted_target for predicted JALs exactly as for
predicted backward branches.

Change jump redirect generation so direct JAL only redirects when the
resolved jump target differs from the predicted next PC. Keep JALR on the
existing always-redirect path, and keep misaligned jump handling as a trap
with no speculative redirect.

Treat the existing predicted_taken/predicted_target fields as generic
control-flow prediction metadata rather than branch-only metadata. No
top-level IO, RVFI width, or architectural state changes are required.

implementation_notes:
# IF Direct-Jump Predictor Implementation Notes

## What changed

- `rtl/if_stage.sv`
  - Added IF-stage recognition for direct JAL (`opcode 1101111`).
  - Added J-immediate reconstruction beside the existing B-immediate logic.
  - Predicts `pc + jal_imm` as the next PC when the target is word-aligned.
  - Emits `predicted_taken` and `predicted_target` for predicted JALs using the existing metadata fields.

- `rtl/ex_stage.sv`
  - Renamed the resolved prediction comparison from branch-specific to generic next-PC prediction logic.
  - Kept conditional branches redirecting only on prediction mismatch.
  - Changed direct JAL redirect generation to mismatch-only: resolved JAL target must differ from the carried predicted next PC.
  - Kept JALR on the always-redirect path.
  - Preserved existing misaligned branch/jump trap behavior: mark the retiring instruction illegal, suppress redirect, and use `pc + 4` as `pc_next`.

- `rtl/core_pkg.sv`
  - Updated comments to describe `predicted_taken` / `predicted_target` as generic control-flow prediction metadata.

## Deviations

- No structural pipeline changes were needed.
- No new top-level IO, RVFI fields, or architectural state were added.
- JALR remains unpredicted as requested.

## Concerns

- The exact in-worktree `bash formal/run_all.sh` command could not run in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to `/Users/bonetto/bonetto/auto-arch-tournament/formal/riscv-formal`, outside the writable roots. The script failed before generating checks while trying to remove staged `.sv` files under that symlink target.
- To still self-check the RTL, I copied the current `rtl/`, `formal/run_all.sh`, `formal/checks.cfg`, `formal/wrapper.sv`, and the riscv-formal tree into `/tmp/aat-formal-run-61907` and ran the same script there.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Exact `bash formal/run_all.sh` in this worktree: blocked by sandbox permissions before proof execution.
- Temporary `/tmp` formal run with the same script and current RTL: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Operand-Aware Load-Use Interlock
motivation:
The current load-use hazard check is deliberately conservative: every load
in ID/EX stalls the next instruction when the load destination matches either
raw rs1 or raw rs2 bitfield in IF/ID. That creates real bubbles for true
load consumers, but it also creates false bubbles for common CoreMark
instructions whose rs fields are immediates or destinations rather than
source operands, especially OP-IMM shifts/arithmetic, LOAD, JAL, LUI, and
AUIPC. The prior full MEM-to-EX load bypass recovered many load-use cycles
but cost too much Fmax because it added a new MEM-data path into EX. A
decode-light operand-use filter should recover the false-stall subset with
almost no new datapath depth and no change to branch prediction, memory
timing, or RVFI retirement.
hypothesis:
Refine the hazard_unit load-use predicate so it only compares the load rd
against source fields that the IF/ID instruction actually reads. Compute
simple uses_rs1 and uses_rs2 bits from the IF/ID opcode: rs1 is used by
OP, OP-IMM, LOAD, STORE, BRANCH, and JALR; rs2 is used only by OP, STORE,
and BRANCH. Keep unknown or reserved opcode classes conservative so symbolic
formal streams and illegal-instruction traps do not lose existing protection.
The pipeline should still insert the exact existing bubble for true load-use
dependencies, but avoid stalling on immediate shamt/imm bits, U/J-type
encodings, and other non-source fields that merely alias the load rd. This
targets iterations/cycle with negligible Fmax risk compared with adding a new
forwarding bus.

changes.description:
Add decode-light source-use detection for the IF/ID instruction and gate
the load_use_hazard comparisons with uses_rs1 and uses_rs2. Preserve the
existing dmem/imem stall behavior, redirect flushing, and conservative
handling for opcode classes that are not clearly source-free.

Pass the IF/ID instruction or its opcode into hazard_unit alongside the
existing rs1/rs2 fields. Do not change the top-level core IO, pipeline
stage boundaries, or the single-retire RVFI channel-0 convention.

title: Two-Entry Retiring Store Buffer
motivation:
The current five-stage core treats every dmem-not-ready cycle on a store as a
full pipeline freeze, even though a store's data and byte mask are already
known in MEM and no register result is needed by younger ALU/control
instructions. With the tournament's random dmem stalls, this turns store bus
backpressure into lost frontend and execute bandwidth. The prior MEM-to-EX
load-use bypass proved that backend bubbles matter, but it put dmem read data
on the EX operand critical path and lost too much Fmax. A small in-order store
buffer targets the store-only part of the same structural bottleneck while
keeping load data, branch compare, and ALU timing out of the new path.
hypothesis:
Add a two-entry FIFO for normal RAM stores. When a legal aligned store to the
RAM address range reaches MEM and the external dmem port is not immediately
available, retire the store into the FIFO with its address, replicated write
data, byte mask, and RVFI metadata, then let younger non-memory instructions
continue through the pipeline while the FIFO drains oldest-first on the dmem
port. Loads, stores when the FIFO is full, and all MMIO/marker/UART stores
must wait until older buffered stores have drained and their own dmem
handshake completes, preserving in-order memory visibility and exact timing
marker behavior. This should recover store-stall cycles under the random
dmem model with much less Fmax risk than a load-result bypass because the
added muxing is confined to the dmem request side and not the EX operand path.

changes.description:
Add a two-entry oldest-first store FIFO with enqueue, full, empty,
drain-valid, and drain-ready controls. Entries hold the byte address,
replicated write data, byte mask, and enough bookkeeping for MEM/WB and
RVFI to retire the store once it has been accepted into the buffer.

Split store handling into bufferable RAM stores and nonbufferable stores.
Bufferable aligned stores may advance to MEM/WB when accepted by the FIFO
even if io_dmemReady is low; loads, misaligned accesses, MMIO stores, and
marker/UART stores keep the current wait-for-handshake behavior. RVFI
mem_addr, mem_wmask, mem_wdata, trap, and valid fields must remain tied to
the retiring instruction, not to the later FIFO drain cycle.

Replace the current any-memory-op dmem hold condition with explicit
backpressure inputs: load waiting for dmem, nonbufferable store waiting
for dmem or older buffered stores, and bufferable store unable to enqueue
because the FIFO is full. Younger non-memory instructions should no
longer be frozen solely because an older buffered store is draining.

Instantiate and arbitrate the store buffer between MEM-stage requests and
the top-level dmem port. Give oldest buffered stores priority when
draining, block younger memory operations behind buffered stores, and
preserve the core IO shape plus the single-retire RVFI channel-0
convention with channel 1 tied off.

Add small packed control fields or typedefs only if needed to carry
bufferable-store, nonbufferable-store, and MEM-stage backpressure state
cleanly between the MEM stage, hazard unit, and top-level arbiter.

title: Static-Biased Branch Agree Predictor
motivation:
The current best frontend already removes most obvious control bubbles by
predicting backward conditional branches taken and direct JAL targets in IF.
The remaining predictor loss is now dominated by conditional branches that
static BTFNT handles poorly: biased forward branches, backward branches with
non-loop behavior, and alias-heavy branch sites in CoreMark's list and state
kernels. A small learned direction predictor can attack those residual
mispredicts without adding a BTB, a target adder beyond the existing branch
immediate path, or any speculative architectural state.
hypothesis:
Add a 64-entry, PC-indexed, two-bit "agree" predictor for conditional branch
direction. IF still computes the existing static BTFNT direction and branch
target from the fetched instruction; the table counter predicts whether the
dynamic outcome will agree with that static direction. Initialize counters to
strongly agree so cold branches behave exactly like the current predictor.
For legal aligned B-type branches, predict taken when the table prediction
combined with the static direction says taken, and carry the existing
predicted_taken/predicted_target metadata into EX. Direct JAL prediction
remains unchanged, and JALR remains unpredicted. When a conditional branch
resolves in EX, update the indexed counter toward agree or disagree with the
static direction exactly once for that branch, and redirect only when the
resolved next PC differs from the predicted next PC. This should recover a
meaningful fraction of the residual CoreMark branch mispredicts while keeping
the IF critical path to an instruction decode, one small table read, the
existing target add, and a next-PC mux.

changes.description:
Add the 64-entry two-bit agree predictor table, indexed by PC[7:2].
Keep the current B-type legality and target-alignment guards. For
conditional branches, combine the table's agree/disagree prediction with
the static BTFNT direction to choose predicted_taken and next_pc. Preserve
the existing direct-JAL prediction path and all redirect/reset/stall
priority rules.

Emit conditional-branch predictor update metadata from the resolved EX
branch: update_valid, update_pc, update_static_taken, and actual_taken.
Gate the update so a held EX instruction caused by dmem backpressure does
not train the table repeatedly. Keep redirect generation based on the
resolved next PC versus the carried predicted next PC.

Wire the EX-stage branch predictor update signals back to IF without
changing the top-level core IO, RVFI channel layout, or single-retire
channel-0 convention.

If needed, add compact predictor-update bundle typedefs or comments for
the IF/EX predictor metadata. Do not alter RVFI fields, architectural
state, or decoder legality behavior.

implementation_notes:
# Static-Biased Branch Agree Predictor

## What changed

- Added a 64-entry, PC[7:2]-indexed, two-bit agree predictor table in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Counters reset to strongly agree (`2'b11`), so cold conditional branches keep the prior BTFNT behavior.
- Conditional branch prediction now combines the static direction (`B` immediate sign) with the agree/disagree prediction while preserving the existing legal funct3 and aligned target guards.
- Direct JAL prediction remains unchanged. JALR remains unpredicted.
- Added EX-to-IF predictor update sideband signals through `rtl/core.sv`.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv` now emits update metadata for valid conditional branches: branch PC, static direction, and actual taken direction.
- Predictor update is gated with `!stall` so a branch held in EX by dmem backpressure does not train repeatedly.

## Deviations

- No `core_pkg.sv` typedef was added; the update path is small enough that separate sideband wires were simpler and avoided changing shared bundle definitions.
- The direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` command could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a location outside the writable sandbox, so its cleanup step failed with `Operation not permitted`. I ran the same script and formal tree from a temporary `/tmp` copy against the current RTL instead.

## Concerns

- No known functional concerns. The predictor can learn an aliased opposite direction, but the existing EX resolved-next-PC comparison still redirects on mismatch.
- IF table reads are combinational and updates are synchronous; same-cycle resolve/fetch of the same table entry uses the old counter value until the next clock edge.

## Local verification

- Lint: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` passed.
- Formal: temp-staged equivalent of `bash formal/run_all.sh` passed: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- Direct worktree formal command was blocked by sandbox permissions before checks started; this was not a counterexample or RTL failure.

title: Hot-Bit ALU Result Mux
motivation:
The current best core is now Fmax-sensitive in the EX-stage ALU path. The
ALU operation encoding is dense across both common RV32I operations and the
RV32M operations, and rtl/alu.sv selects every result through one broad case
statement. Recent experiments showed that touching the multiplier primitive
itself is risky: the 33x33 shared-multiplier rewrite preserved IPC but badly
hurt Fmax. A safer micro-optimization is to leave every arithmetic primitive
and pipeline handshake unchanged, but make the ALU control encoding expose a
hot/cold split so synthesis can build a shallower result mux for the common
integer/address/branch work.
hypothesis:
Re-encode ALU op constants so bit 4 is a registered "M-extension operation"
discriminator: all RV32I/simple operations keep op[4]=0, while MUL/MULH/
MULHSU/MULHU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU move to op[4]=1 with op[2:0] matching the
RV32M funct3 order. Refactor rtl/alu.sv into a hot simple-result mux for
ADD/SUB/logic/compare/shift/LUI and a separate M-result mux for the existing
multiply/divide/remainder expressions, then select between them with op[4].
This should reduce LUT depth and routing on the common EX/MEM alu_result
path without changing instruction latency, CoreMark cycle count, RV32M bit
semantics, branch prediction, memory behavior, or the RVFI retirement
contract. The RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions remain present and keyed to
the same M operations under the new encoding.

changes.description:
Reassign ALU_MUL through ALU_REMU to a contiguous op[4]=1 encoding,
preferably 5'b10000 through 5'b10111 in RV32M funct3 order. Keep all
non-M ALU operations at op[4]=0 so the bit can be used directly as the
hot/cold ALU result select. Do not change bundle widths, RVFI fields, or
architectural control semantics.

Split the single broad result case into simple_out and m_out sub-muxes.
simple_out should cover ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR/SLT/SLTU/SLL/SRL/SRA/LUI;
m_out should reuse the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU
arithmetic, including all divide-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 corner cases.
Drive out with op[4] ? m_out : simple_out, and keep the ALTOPS formulas
under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS with the new M op constants.
